segments.s518-g9: بزرگان (subject) + relative clause introduced by که. از تخم آرش is a genitival/origin phrase 'from the seed of Arash' (تخم = 'seed') and بدند is the past plural copular/verb 'they were' placed at the end; the clause therefore reads 'the great ones who were of Arash's seed.' The verb بدند is an older/poetic past plural form.
segments.s518-g10: A coordinated nominal/adjectival predicate: three qualities (دلیر و سبکسار و سرکش) are listed before the verb بدند, which again is the past plural 'they were' and agrees with بزرگان. The coordinating و simply links the adjectives; the verb at the end is typical of Persian word order in poetry.
segments.s518-g11: A locative framing: به گیتی 'in/throughout the world' followed by به هر گوشه‌ای 'in every corner' and then بر یکی 'over/for one' — i.e. 'in the world, in every corner there was one (person)'. Word order is emphatic/poetic: place phrases first, indefinite one (یکی) after.
segments.s518-g12: گرفته is a past participle/verb 'taken' (had taken/held), ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from each country' (ز هر کشوری), and اندکی is the quantifier 'a few / a little' placed at the end to quantify what was taken. Together: 'having taken a few from every country.'
segments.s518-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بر تختشان 'upon their throne' is the locative/goal phrase; شاد 'joyful/merry' here functions adverbially modifying the action; بنشاندند is the past plural transitive verb 'they seated/installed' placed at the end. The direct object (him) is omitted but implied by the transitive verb.
segments.s518-g14: ملوک طوایف is the subject 'the kings of the tribes' (noun + genitive/attributive), همی is an archaic/emphatic particle often used in epic verse (here marking the narrative action), and خواندند is the past plural 'they called/summoned' (verb at sentence end).
segments.s518-g15: برین گونه is a fixed phrase 'in this manner/thus' (بر + این/ین + گونه), بگذشت is the past 'passed', and سالی دویست is a poetic, inverted measure phrase meaning 'two hundred years' (سالی used as a measure). Word order is poetic (manner + verb + time-quantity).
segments.s518-g16: تو گفتی is the simple past 'you said' (گفتی), که introduces the reported clause, اندر زمین 'in the land/earth', شاه 'king' and نیست 'is not/there is not' form the subordinate predication. Note that classical narrative often uses present-form نیست in reported content to state a general/continuing absence.
segments.s518-g17: نکردند is a negated past plural verb (نَ + کرد + -ند) meaning ‘they did not do/they did not make,’ here governing یاد as its object: یاد ... نکردند = ‘they did not remember.’ The string این ازان آن ازین is a series of demonstratives/particles (این, ازان, آن, ازین) listed in compressed, poetic order — essentially ‘this, that, those, from this’ — functioning as the items they failed to remember.
segments.s518-g18: برآسود is a past/inchoative verbal adjective ‘was at rest/was relieved’ (literary/poetic form). یک چند is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘for a little while’ or ‘a short time.’ روی زمین is a locative phrase ‘upon the earth / on earth.’ Together they form the second clause: ‘for a short while some on earth were at rest.’
segments.s518-g19: سکندر is the proper name ‘Alexander’ (subject). سگالید is a past verb (literary) meaning ‘set on/assailed/put into action’ — here used of carrying out a plan; زین‌گونه رای is a noun phrase ‘this kind of counsel/plan’ (زین‌گونه = ‘thus/so’; رای = ‘plan, counsel’). Word order is Subject + Verb + object (poetic but transparent).
segments.s518-g20: که ... ماند is a subordinate clause introduced by که + تا: که تا here gives purpose/result sense (‘so that/in order that’) and ماند is the past tense ‘remained/lasted.’ روم آباد ماند به جای = ‘Rome remained prosperous in its place.’ The sequence places the purpose/result after the main clause, a typical classical arrangement.
segments.s518-g21: نخست is an adverb ‘first/at first.’ اشک is a proper name (Ashk) and بود is the past copula ‘was,’ so the clause reads ‘First was Ashk.’ از نژاد قباد is a genitive phrase ‘of the lineage/stock of Qobad,’ qualifying Ashk. The copula can be elliptical for subsequent names.
segments.s518-g22: دگر here means ‘another/next’ and introduces the next group of names; the verb (بود) is omitted but understood from the previous clause, a common poetic ellipsis. گرد functions here as a proper name (an individual), followed by شاپور and خسرو (proper names); نژاد at the end again indicates ‘lineage/stock.’
segments.s518-g23: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of.’ ز یک دست is an idiomatic phrase meaning ‘of one house/one hand’ (here ‘of the same house/line’); دست literally ‘hand’ is used idiomatically. گودرز is the proper name (Goudarz) and اشکانیان is an appositive/genitive ‘the Parthians,’ marking his ethnic/tribal affiliation: ‘Goudarz, of the Parthians, from the same house.’
segments.s518-g24: چو introduces a comparison ‘like/as’; بیژن is the name Bijan. که بود introduces a relative clause ‘who was…’ and از نژاد کیان states his pedigree ‘of the lineage of the Kians’ (کیان = the Kian royal house). Word order here is the common Classical Persian pattern: comparative + noun + relative clause giving the parallel example.
segments.s518-g25: چو here is the comparitive particle 'like/as'. نرسی is a personal name and functions as the example being compared (not the verb 'to arrive'); و simply coordinates the two examples; the second چون repeats 'like' before اورمزد بزرگ ('the great Ohrmazd'). The adjective بزرگ follows the name, as is normal in Persian, giving the epithet 'the great'.
segments.s518-g26: This repeats the comparative چـو 'like' and names آرش (Arash). که introduces a relative clause 'who/which…'. بد here precedes نامدار and is classical/poetic: it gives a negative or emphatic shade to the following adjective (literally 'bad/ill‑reputed'); نامدار is the adjective 'renowned'. سترگ is a poetic noun meaning 'sight/eye' and describes what Arash was famed for (his keen/strong sight).
segments.s518-g27: چو here functions temporally as 'when'. ز (here contracted as زو = 'از او') is the poetic short form meaning 'from/of him' or 'by him'; together چو زو بگذری literally 'when you pass by him'. بگذری is the present/subjunctive 2nd‑person form used generically ('you pass' / 'if/when you pass'). نامدار and اردوان form the noun phrase 'the famed Ardavan'.
segments.s518-g28: A string of epithets joined by و: خردمند 'wise' is a simple adjective; با رای literally 'with counsel' is an adjectival/qualifying phrase meaning 'of good counsel' (رای here = 'plan/counsel'); روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑soul/clear‑minded'. The coordination links these qualities as attributes of اردوان.
segments.s518-g29: چو here = 'when'. بنشست is the past tense 'sat (down)' used idiomatically for 'took his seat/assumed power'; بهرام is the subject. ز اشکانیان ('from/among the Ashkanians = Parthians') indicates origin or group affiliation. Word order places the temporal phrase before the subject-verb clause.
segments.s518-g30: ببخشید is the past tense of بخشیدن and here means 'he gave/bestowed'. The object گنجی 'a treasure' appears before the recipient بارزانیان (the Barzanians); Persian often places the object before the indirect object or beneficiary without an overt preposition. The verb is transitive and governs both noun phrases.
segments.s518-g31: ورا is the accusative/clitic-like form meaning 'him/to him' placed before the verb for emphasis. خواندند is past plural and here means 'they called/named' (i.e., gave him the name/title). اردوان بزرگ is the name plus epithet 'Ardavan the Great' functioning as the name they gave.
segments.s518-g32: که introduces a relative clause 'who…'. از marks the source/thing from which something is taken ('from the ewe'); میش is 'ewe/sheep'. بگسست is past tense 'tore off/broke away' (here transitive: he tore off); چنگال گرگ 'the wolf's talon/claw' is the thing torn away. The word order is object (چنگال گرگ) after the verb but the clause overall means 'who tore the wolf's talon from the ewe'.
segments.s518-g1: کنون‌ای is an emphatic vocative: کنون (now) combined with ای (O) addressing someone. سراینده فرتوت مرد is a string of nouns/adjectives in apposition — سراینده (the singer/poet) modified by فرتوت (aged, decrepit) and then qualified by مرد (man). The whole phrase is a vocative address: 'O aged poet-man.'
segments.s518-g2: سوی introduces a direction/target ('toward' or 'to') and takes the following noun phrase. گاه اشکانیان here means 'the time/era of the Parthians' (گاه = time + genitive). بازگرد is an imperative (return/come back) — a present/imperative poetic form asking the addressee to go back in time/context.
segments.s518-g3: چه گفت is an interrogative phrase 'what said' / 'what does (it) say' (elliptical phrasing common in classical Persian). اندر is the preposition 'in/within'. آن نامه راستان = 'that truthful/true letter' with راستان postposed as an adjective/qualifier of نامه.
segments.s518-g4: که is the relative 'which/that'. گوینده is the narrator/speaker acting as subject. یاد آرد is a fixed verbal phrase: یاد (remembrance) + آرد (brings) — together 'brings to mind, recalls'. از باستان is a prepositional phrase 'from antiquity' with باستان used as a noun 'antiquity.' The verb آرد is third-person present/habitual narrative.
segments.s518-g5: پس از is the compound preposition 'after'. روزگار سکندر is a genitive phrase 'the time/age of Alexander'. جهان precedes the verb as the subject meaning 'the world/people' and will link to the interrogative that follows.
segments.s518-g6: چه گوید is the interrogative 'what says' (گوید here is a narrative/present form 'says'); کرا is an archaic interrogative 'who'. بود is the past copula 'was' (placing the question in past narrative). تخت مهان is the noun phrase 'throne ...' with مهان a following modifier; the segment asks 'who was (on) the throne.'
segments.s518-g7: چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = so/thus; گفت = said). داننده دهقان چاچ is a nominal phrase: داننده 'the knowing/learned one' qualifying دهقان (the dehqan, a landed gentry/farmer), followed by چاچ indicating origin ('of Chach'). Word order places titles and origin after the head noun in this poetic line.
segments.s518-g8: کزان پس is 'from which/thereafter' linking back to the previous statement. کسی را نبد is an archaic negative construction meaning 'there was no one' (نبد = was not/existed not), with را functioning as the object/definite marker in this impersonal expression. تخت عاج = 'throne of ivory' (تخت + عاج), so the clause says that thereafter no one possessed/sat upon an ivory throne.
segments.s518-g33: ورا is an adverbial demonstrative here meaning 'there' or 'it lay there'; بود is the past tense of بودن ('was/lay'); شیراز تا اصفهان is a locative span 'Shiraz to Isfahan' with تا functioning as 'as far as'/'up to'. Word order places the locative phrase after the copula, a typical poetic arrangement.
segments.s518-g34: که introduces a relative/causal clause 'which/that'; داننده is an agentive noun 'the knower' or 'wise man'; خواندش is a contracted classical form 'خواند + ش' meaning 'he called it' (here 'call/name'); مرز is 'border/frontier'; مهان is a substantive meaning 'the chiefs/visitors' (here the group whose frontier is meant). The clause explains or names the stretch just mentioned.
segments.s518-g35: به اصطخر is a locative phrase 'at/into Istakhr' (به used here to mark location/setting); بد before a name is an adjectival epithet 'ill/bad' or 'miserable'; بابک is the personal name; از دست اوی literally 'from his hand'—دست here means 'control/hand' (power), and اوی is an older possessive/3rd-person reference 'his'. The whole segment says Babak was in bad hands/under bad control at Istakhr.
segments.s518-g36: که introduces an explanatory/casual clause 'for/because'; تنین خروشان is a compound nominal phrase 'a roaring, thunderous sound' (تنین = deep sound/thunder, خروشان = roaring); بد از شست اوی literally 'bad from his shast/thumb' — poetically this means 'a dreadful roar issued from his hand' where شست (thumb) is used metonymically for the hand/action. The clause gives the reason for Babak's ill state.
segments.s518-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; کوتاه شد is a verbal phrase 'became short / was cut short' (کوتاه = short, شد = became/was), with the adjective before the copula-like verb; شاخ 'branch' is the thing shortened; و هم بیخشان adds 'and also their roots'—بیخ + ـشان is 'root(s) of them/their roots'.
segments.s518-g38: نگوید here is an older third-person negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not say' / 'does not recount' (classical narrative usage); جهاندار is a title 'the sovereign' or 'world-ruler'; تاریخشان 'their history' (تاریخ + possessive suffix شان). The clause states that the ruler does not speak their history after their branches and roots are cut.
segments.s518-g39: کزیشان is a fused classical particle meaning 'from/of them' (کز = از + که kind of contraction, here 'from them'); جز 'except'; از نام 'of the name' (i.e. 'only by name'); نشنیده‌ام is the first-person perfect negative 'I have not heard' or 'I have not heard of' (ن + شنیده‌ام). The clause says the speaker knows them only by name.
segments.s518-g40: نه ... دیده‌ام is a continuation with negation 'nor have I seen'; در نامه خسروان is a locative-possessive phrase 'in the records/books of the kings' (در = in; نامه used here as 'record/document'/'book'; خسروان = 'the kings'); دیده‌ام is 'I have seen'. Together: 'Nor have I seen them in the kings' annals.'
segments.s518-g41: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو = “when.” سکندر is the subject and چو ... گشت is a clause meaning “when (he) became ...”; نومید is an adjective used predicatively and گشت (past of گشتن) is the copular/auxiliary meaning “became.” از جهان is a prepositional complement ‘of/from the world,’ so the whole segment = “when Alexander became hopeless of the world.”
segments.s518-g42: بیفگند is a past action verb (he cast/threw), here used as the main verb; رایی is its direct object meaning ‘a counsel/opinion/plan.’ میان مهان is a prepositional phrase ‘among the (great) men/guests’ that locates where the counsel was cast. Word order (verb + object + adjunct) is poetic but straightforward.
segments.s518-g43: بدان is an adverbial/phrase ‘by/with that’ (here formed from the prefix ب- + دان as a fused item meaning ‘by that’); تا introduces a purpose/result clause ‘so that.’ نگیرد is a negative jussive/subjunctive/present-form meaning ‘should not take/seize’; کس follows the negative and functions as ‘no one’ here. از روم is the source ‘from Rum/Byzantium,’ and یاد is the object ‘remembrance/memory.’ The whole clause expresses purpose/result: ‘so that no one from Rum would seize the memory.’
segments.s518-g44: بماند is a present/jussive form (ب- + ماند) used here to express that something ‘remains’ or be allowed to remain; it functions as the main verb of this result clause. مران is a proper name modifying کشور in a poetic order (the realm of Meran), and آباد و شاد are coordinating adjectives ‘prosperous and happy.’ The segment means ‘Meran’s country remained prosperous and joyful.’
segments.s518-g45: چو = ‘when’ introduces a temporal clause. دانا is a predicate adjective ‘wise,’ and بود is the past copula ‘was.’ بر زمین is the locative phrase ‘on the earth/land,’ and شهریار is the noun ‘ruler/king’ (the subject of the clause). Word order places the predicate before the subject in poetic style: ‘When a wise king was on the earth…’
segments.s518-g46: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so.’ آورد is the past tense ‘brought’ (third person singular). دانش is the grammatical subject ‘knowledge,’ placed before the verb as is common in poetry. شاه بار is a compound where شاه (‘king’) modifies بار (‘burden/responsibility’), so the object/phrase means ‘the kingly burden/responsibility.’ Together: ‘thus knowledge brought the kingly burden.’

sentences.s518-s5: The great ones who sprang from Arash's seed were brave, light‑hearted, and rebellious.
sentences.s518-s6: Across the world, in every corner there was one who had taken a few (from each country).
sentences.s518-s7: When they joyfully set him upon their throne, the tribal kings summoned him.
sentences.s518-s8: Thus passed two hundred years; you said that there is no king in the land.
sentences.s518-s9: They remembered neither this nor that nor any of these things; for a short while a few on earth were at rest.
sentences.s518-s10: Alexander put this counsel into effect, so that Rome might remain prosperous in its place.
sentences.s518-s11: First was Ashk, of the lineage of Qobad; then (came) Gard, Shapur, and Khosrow of that noble stock.
sentences.s518-s12: Goudarz of the Parthians was of the same house, like Bijan, who was of the Kian lineage.
sentences.s518-s13: Like Nersi and like the great Ohrmazd; like Arash, who — though ill‑reputed — was famed for his mighty sight.
sentences.s518-s14: When you pass by him, the famed Ardavan — wise, of good counsel, and clear‑souled.
sentences.s518-s15: When Bahram sat among the Parthians, he bestowed a treasure on the Barzanians.
sentences.s518-s16: They called him Ardavan the Great — he who tore the wolf's talon from the ewe.
sentences.s518-s1: Now, O aged minstrel, return to the time of the Parthians.
sentences.s518-s2: What did that true letter say, which the narrator brings to mind from antiquity?
sentences.s518-s3: Then, after Alexander's time, what does the world say — who was upon the throne?
sentences.s518-s4: Thus said the knowing dehqan of Chach: from that time on no one had a throne of ivory.
sentences.s518-s17: It lay from Shiraz to Isfahan, which the knower called the frontier of the chiefs.
sentences.s518-s18: At Istakhr Babak fared badly in his hands, for a roaring, thunderous sound issued from his (hand).
sentences.s518-s19: When their branches were cut short and their roots likewise, the sovereign does not recount their history.
sentences.s518-s20: Of them I have heard nothing but the name; nor have I seen them in the records of the kings.
sentences.s518-s21: When Alexander grew hopeless of the world, he cast a counsel among the nobles.
sentences.s518-s22: By that (act) — so that no one from Rum would seize the memory — Meran’s realm remained prosperous and joyful.
sentences.s518-s23: When a wise sovereign was on the earth, thus knowledge brought the kingly burden.
